our_argument_summary: Our review states that westward drift of the SAA is well-tracked by WMM2025 and IGRF-14, explained by core dynamics, and that the dome model adopts a 400+ year observational fact as a new V51.0 WIN without any derivation from dome geometry. Verdict: Std Model Explains.

relevant_files: raw-text/03-predictions.txt (W022 = WIN-040 position check, not WIN-060 drift)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt (SAA Decay Rate domain — no separate 'western cell shift' domain)
raw-text/02-wins.txt (no WIN-060 appears in the dome's V50.6-era wins page)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No distinct monitoring domain exists for westward drift of the SAA western cell. The dome's W022 prediction ('SAA western cell position: West of 45 deg W, cells separated') corresponds to WIN-040 (static position threshold), not WIN-060 (temporal drift direction). The live dashboard's SAA domain tracks decay rate (77.0 nT/yr), not drift velocity or direction. WIN-060 has no automated check in the monitoring infrastructure.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring exists for this WIN. The dome site does not appear to track or validate western cell drift direction as a separate domain. The 'SAA Decay Rate' domain on the live dashboard is about intensity decrease, not positional drift.
data_sources_used: ESA Swarm L1B magnetometer (cited in our evidence)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: No explicit escape clause. The dome claims westward drift is consistent with 'aetheric field rotation' but provides no quantitative prediction of drift rate, direction, or acceleration to test.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: WIN-060 is tagged monitoring='hardcoded' in wins.json, but no hardcoded check for westward drift exists in the dome's monitoring infrastructure. The W022 prediction is for WIN-040 (position threshold), not WIN-060 (drift direction). This WIN should be monitoring='none', matching WIN-040 and the rest of the group.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No domain in the live dashboard or predictions tracker corresponds to 'SAA western cell drift direction.' W022 ('SAA western cell position: West of 45 deg W, cells separated') maps to WIN-040 (static position check), not WIN-060. The SAA Decay Rate domain tracks intensity (nT/yr), not positional drift. No separate monitoring for this WIN exists.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Westward drift of geomagnetic field features is standard geophysics, explained by differential rotation between Earth's outer core and mantle (core-mantle coupling). Published extensively since Bullard et al. (1950) and documented in every IGRF model. The dome model renames the mechanism as 'aetheric field rotation' but predicts no different numerical drift rate.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Westward drift of magnetic declination has been observed and documented for over 400 years, since Halley's magnetic charts (1701). Modern satellite confirmation via Swarm and CHAMP predates the dome model's V51.0 by decades. This is a centuries-old observational fact repackaged as a new V51.0 WIN.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation from dome geometry (H(r), disc radius, aetheric parameters) yields a prediction of westward drift rate or direction. The dome site provides no MHD-equivalent equations for aetheric flow that would produce directional drift. The observation is adopted from geomagnetic surveys, not derived from dome physics.

holes_found.description: Monitoring tag mismatch: WIN-060 is tagged monitoring='hardcoded' in wins.json, but no hardcoded monitoring check exists for this WIN. The W022 prediction maps to WIN-040 (static position 'west of 45°W'), not WIN-060 (drift direction). All other WINs in this group (WIN-040, 041, 043, 059, 063) are correctly tagged 'none.' WIN-060's 'hardcoded' tag is an error from Cycle 1 that propagated into wins.json.
holes_found.recommendation: Change WIN-060's code_analysis.monitoring from 'hardcoded' to 'none' to match the rest of its group and the actual monitoring infrastructure.

holes_found.description: Cycle 1 recommendation not applied: The Cycle 1 review recommended adding a clear distinction between WIN-004 (separation between cells), WIN-005 (decay rate), and WIN-060 (drift direction) to preempt the dome defender's argument that these test independent physical properties. The current text still says 'overlaps data used in WIN-004/005' without explaining why these overlapping uses of the same data constitute redundancy rather than independent tests.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence to detail_evidence clarifying: 'While separation (WIN-004), intensity decay (WIN-005), and drift direction (WIN-060) describe different aspects of SAA evolution, all three are read from the same INTERMAGNET/Swarm field maps. The dome model derives none of these properties from its geometry — it observes all three from the same dataset and counts each observation as a separate WIN.'

holes_found.description: Summary-table finding is dense and may overstate the argument. The finding reads: 'Non-discriminating: dome extrapolates same drift data as NOAA/ESA; geomagnetic drift explained by standard core-mantle dynamics without any dome-specific parameters.' This is accurate but front-loads 'Non-discriminating' which is our framing, not the dome's claim. A defender could say we're strawmanning by dismissing their observation before engaging with it.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider rephrasing the finding to lead with the factual observation before our verdict framing: 'Westward drift documented since Halley (1701); dome adopts known observation with no geometric derivation of drift rate or direction.'

holes_found.description: The detail_claim is extremely terse: 'SAA western cell drifts westward as predicted.' This accurately paraphrases the dome's claim, but 'as predicted' begs the question: predicted by whom, when, and from what derivation? Our evidence addresses this but the claim field should reflect what the dome actually asserts. If the dome doesn't use the word 'predicted' for this specific WIN, we shouldn't put it in their mouth.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the dome's exact wording. If the dome says 'confirmed' rather than 'predicted,' the detail_claim should reflect that: 'SAA western cell drifts westward as confirmed by observation.'

holes_found.description: The duplicate sentence in WIN-040's detail_evidence ('Notably, the dome's own predictions tracker listed W022... meaning the dome itself had not confirmed this WIN at baseline.') appears twice verbatim. This is a copy-paste artifact in a sibling WIN, not in WIN-060 itself, but readers viewing the group may notice.
holes_found.recommendation: Fix the duplicate sentence in WIN-040's detail_evidence (not WIN-060). This is a WIN-040 issue but flagging it here since it affects the group.

stronger_arguments.description: Quantify the dome's prediction gap: standard MHD models (e.g., Finlay et al. 2010, 2016) predict westward drift rates of ~0.2°/year for the SAA, derived from core flow inversions. The dome model provides no equivalent quantitative prediction. This makes the asymmetry concrete rather than abstract.
sources: Finlay et al., 2010, 'International Geomagnetic Reference Field: the eleventh generation', Geophys. J. Int.
Terra-Nova et al., 2019, 'Relating the South Atlantic Anomaly and geomagnetic flux patches', PEPI

stronger_arguments.description: The V51.0 timing is itself evidence of post-hoc retrofitting: WIN-060 is new_in_v51=true, yet westward drift is one of the oldest observations in geomagnetism. Including a 400-year-old observation as a 'new WIN' in 2026 is the definition of post-hoc retrodiction. Our evidence mentions this but doesn't emphasize the absurdity of the timeline.
sources: Halley, E. (1692), 'An account of the cause of the change of the variation of the magnetical needle', Phil. Trans. R. Soc.

kernel_of_truth.description: The westward drift of the SAA western cell is real, well-documented, and ongoing. Any model of geomagnetic field evolution must be consistent with this observation. The dome model correctly identifies this as an important observable property of the field.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Consistency with a known observation is necessary but not sufficient for a scientific prediction. The dome model provides no geometric derivation that independently predicts the drift rate, direction, or acceleration. Standard MHD models derive drift from core flow; the dome model simply observes it. Capturing a 400-year-old observation does not constitute predictive power.

deeper_analysis: WIN-060 exemplifies the dome model's V51.0 WIN-inflation strategy: take well-established geophysical observations, reframe them as dome 'predictions,' and count each separately. The SAA group (WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063) represents 8 WINs from essentially the same dataset (INTERMAGNET/Swarm geomagnetic field maps). None of these 8 WINs derives a quantitative prediction from dome geometry — they all observe known phenomena and declare confirmation. The monitoring tag error (hardcoded→none) is significant because it inflates the count of WINs with 'active monitoring' when in fact this WIN has no automated check at all. Cycle 1 correctly identified the citation weakness (generic NOAA URL) and this was fixed — the current text properly cites WMM2025, IGRF-14, and Swarm L1B. The remaining weakness is the lack of explicit distinction between the overlapping WIN-004/005/060 observables, which a dome defender could exploit by arguing these test independent physical properties. Our counter should be: 'independent properties read from the same dataset without independent derivation does not multiply predictive power.' The verdict 'Std Model Explains' is correct and well-supported — standard core-mantle dynamics fully explain westward drift with quantitative predictions from MHD inversions, while the dome model offers no equivalent calculation.

citations_verified: WMM2025 (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model) — Verified: correct URL for NOAA's World Magnetic Model
IGRF-14 (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/international-geomagnetic-reference-field) — Verified: correct URL for IGRF product page
ESA Swarm (https://earth.esa.int/eogateway/missions/swarm) — Verified: correct URL for the Swarm mission page
Edmund Halley 17th-century declination surveys — Factually correct: Halley published magnetic charts and the first declination chart in 1701
citations_unchecked: Whether INTERMAGNET network is directly cited with URL — the detail_evidence mentions INTERMAGNET indirectly via 'derived from the INTERMAGNET global observatory network' but no direct INTERMAGNET link is provided for WIN-060 (though INTERMAGNET appears via the group rebuttal in WIN-040)
